mechanism_to_validate: Hospital demand is visible, urgent and politically costly; upstream access failure is dispersed and delayed.
A single entity commissions hospital, primary and community services, but hospital operational risk is more immediate.
Capitation supports continuity but weakly funds additional clinically necessary contacts.
Patients choose among waiting, paying, delaying, telehealth, ambulance and ED.
PHOs may add population-health value but payment-gateway functions may add friction or opacity.
ACC activity funding may stabilise primary care capacity and shift pressure across funders.
Ambulance may default to ED conveyance unless safe alternatives are funded and accountable.
Funding eligibility may be narrower than safe clinical scope, creating artificial professional bottlenecks.
Telehealth extends access but may weaken local in-person supply if not integrated.
Co-payments can signal demand but can deter necessary care and worsen equity.
What is elevated to top-tier reporting gets managed; upstream failures may otherwise remain residual.
Direct benefits can expand access but do not replace trust, outreach, kaupapa Maori/Pacific functions.
Reform can be framed as pro-market, anti-PHO, anti-GP, pro-patient or anti-equity.
Hidden unmet need remains less fundable if it is not measured and linked to downstream outcomes.
